title: Acute mental health benefits of urban nature

data_scope.spatial_coverage: global (studies mapped worldwide; unique city/country count not reported as a single figure)
data_scope.primary_data: extracted from previously published studies (no new fieldwork)

core_conclusion: A systematic review of 449 studies and meta-analysis of 78 field experiments show that short-term exposure to urban nature provides substantial, broad-spectrum benefits across 12 mental-health outcomes. Green spaces such as urban forests and parks most strongly reduce negative moods (depression, anxiety) and enhance well-being; benefits are largest in young adults but consistently positive across all ages.
why_it_matters: Converts scattered field experiments into a quantitative effect-size synthesis; ranks urban forests and parks as the strongest beneficial nature types; identifies young adults as benefiting most; signals a duration dose-response; and is fully open and reproducible via GitHub.
why_nature: Global, transferable evidence rather than a single-city study; couples urban environment to human well-being; high policy translatability for green infrastructure and public health; rigorous PRISMA + random-effects methodology with open data and code.
added_value_to_my_research: Geolocate effect sizes and fit MGWR meta-regression to map nature-mental-health heterogeneity
Couple per-exposure benefit with 2SFCA/R2SFCA green-space accessibility for realized population gain
Formalize a satellite-computable greenness-dose function for explainable spatial prediction
Validate acute effects against longitudinal GPS/wearable/mobile-phone exposure via fusion + ST-KDE
Benefit-weighted facility-location siting of green space under efficiency-equity trade-offs
